ilike-case-insensitive.label: Case-Insensitive Matching
ilike-case-insensitive.summary: Learn how to perform case-insensitive searches in SQL using ILIKE or similar operators. This is useful for matching text regardless of letter case, such as finding names or cities in a customer database.

intro-ilike.title: Why Case-Insensitive Matching Matters
intro-ilike.bodyMarkdown:
When searching for text in SQL, the standard `LIKE` operator is case-sensitive in many databases. This means `LIKE 'Chicago'` will not match `chicago` or `CHICAGO`. To match text regardless of case, use `ILIKE` (in PostgreSQL) or functions like `LOWER()` in SQLite. This is especially helpful when searching for names, cities, or emails where users may enter data with different capitalization.

**Example:**

Suppose you want to find all customers from Chicago, regardless of how 'Chicago' is capitalized in the `city` column:

```sql
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE LOWER(city) = 'chicago';
```

This query converts all values in the `city` column to lowercase before comparing them to `'chicago'`, ensuring a case-insensitive match.

ilike-vs-like.title: ILIKE vs LIKE and Alternatives
ilike-vs-like.bodyMarkdown:
Some SQL dialects, like PostgreSQL, support the `ILIKE` operator for case-insensitive pattern matching. However, SQLite (used in this dataset) does not support `ILIKE`. Instead, you can use the `LOWER()` or `UPPER()` functions to normalize both sides of the comparison.

**Pattern Matching Example:**

To find customers whose email ends with `@example.com`, regardless of case:

```sql
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE LOWER(email) LIKE '%@example.com';
```

This ensures emails like `MIA@EXAMPLE.COM` or `mia@example.com` are both matched.

single-choice-ilike.title: Case-Insensitive Search in SQLite
single-choice-ilike.prompt: Which SQL expression will find all customers whose city is 'chicago', regardless of capitalization, in SQLite?
single-choice-ilike.hint: Think about how to make both sides lowercase.

help.concept: SQLite does not support ILIKE, so you need to use a function to normalize case.
help.hint_1: Use the LOWER() function on the column.
help.hint_2: Compare LOWER(city) to the lowercase string.

multi-choice-patterns.title: Matching Emails Case-Insensitively
multi-choice-patterns.prompt: Select all SQL expressions that will match emails ending with '@example.com', regardless of case, in SQLite.
multi-choice-patterns.hint: You need to transform the email column to match the pattern's case.

help.concept: To match text case-insensitively in SQLite, use LOWER() or UPPER() on the column and pattern.
help.hint_1: Convert the email column to lower or upper case before comparing.
help.hint_2: ILIKE is not supported in SQLite, so use LOWER() or UPPER().

drag-reorder-case.title: Steps for Case-Insensitive Comparison
drag-reorder-case.prompt: Arrange the steps to perform a case-insensitive comparison in SQLite.
drag-reorder-case.hint: Think about the order: select, transform, prepare value, compare.

help.concept: Case-insensitive comparison involves transforming both sides to the same case before comparing.
help.hint_1: Start with the SELECT, then normalize the column.
help.hint_2: Lowercase the value before comparing with =.

tokens.t2: Write the comparison value in lowercase

fill-blank-choice-ilike.title: Fill in the Function
fill-blank-choice-ilike.prompt:
Fill in the blank to make this WHERE clause case-insensitive in SQLite:

```sql
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE ____ (city) = 'miami';
```

help.concept: the missing term() converts text to lowercase, which helps with case-insensitive matching.
help.hint_1: You want to compare both sides in lowercase.
help.hint_2: the missing term() is the function for this.

code-input-case-insensitive.title: Find All Customers Named 'Emma Reed' (Any Case)
code-input-case-insensitive.prompt: Write a SQL query to select all columns for customers whose full_name is 'Emma Reed', regardless of case or extra spaces. Use SQLite functions as needed.
code-input-case-insensitive.hint: Use LOWER() and TRIM() to normalize the full_name column.

help.concept: To match names regardless of case and spaces, use LOWER() to ignore case and TRIM() to remove extra spaces.
help.hint_1: Apply TRIM() to remove spaces, then LOWER() to ignore case.
help.hint_2: Compare the normalized column to the lowercase, trimmed name.
